coredata.dc:title: A review of effects and applications of ultrasound in mineral flotation
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yuran
Truong, Vu N.T.
Bu, Xiangning
Xie, Guangyuan
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasound technology is widely applied in the flotation process. From the perspective of the theory of ultrasound, this article explains the effects and applications of ultrasound in the flotation process. To obtain a clear understanding of ultrasonic effects, we observe the phenomena of ultrasound using a high-speed camera and a CCD camera, and investigate potential applications in flotation. From these different phenomena, the ultrasonic effects are classified into three types of effect: the transient cavitation effect, stable cavitation effect, and acoustic radiation force effect. Based on these effects, the applications of ultrasound to mineral flotation are reviewed, including slime coating removal, oxidation film removal, desulfuration, tiny bubble generation, flotation reagent dispersion, and aggregation. In addition, the ultrasonic equipment and treatment methods applied in flotation are classified and compared based on their characteristics. Finally, we propose some potential directions in the study of the stable cavitation effect and acoustic radiation force effect, which are important, but are seldom mentioned in previous reports.

Acoustic radiation force

head.ce:title: A review of effects and applications of ultrasound in mineral flotation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasound technology is widely applied in the flotation process. From the perspective of the theory of ultrasound, this article explains the effects and applications of ultrasound in the flotation process. To obtain a clear understanding of ultrasonic effects, we observe the phenomena of ultrasound using a high-speed camera and a CCD camera, and investigate potential applications in flotation. From these different phenomena, the ultrasonic effects are classified into three types of effect: the transient cavitation effect, stable cavitation effect, and acoustic radiation force effect. Based on these effects, the applications of ultrasound to mineral flotation are reviewed, including slime coating removal, oxidation film removal, desulfuration, tiny bubble generation, flotation reagent dispersion, and aggregation. In addition, the ultrasonic equipment and treatment methods applied in flotation are classified and compared based on their characteristics. Finally, we propose some potential directions in the study of the stable cavitation effect and acoustic radiation force effect, which are important, but are seldom mentioned in previous reports.

ce:section.ce:para: Flotation is a conventional separation method that is employed to recover valuable fine minerals from mining ores. High recovery and grade are important requirements for mineral resources and our living environment. In some conditions, flotation results commonly do not meet the industrial requirements, owing to factors such as the fine mineral size, water entrainment, slime coating, and surface oxidation [1–3]. The ultra-fine particle size is the most challenging problem in the flotation process owing to the low probability of collision with conventional flotation-sized bubbles [4,5]. Furthermore, the oxidation films on minerals render their surface as more hydrophilic, leading to a decrease in the adhesion probability and an increase in the detachment probability [2,6]. In addition, the entrainment of clay or gangue particles usually deteriorates the grade of the concentrate, which remains a major challenge in the mineral flotation process [1]. To overcome these problems, for many years, ultrasonic technologies have been studied to improve flotation efficiency. However, there remains a limited fundamental understanding of how ultrasound can influence flotation.

Sound waves are generally defined as simple longitudinal pressure waves that travel through a medium [7]. It is considered as an ultrasound wave when the frequency is higher than 20 kHz. Key advantages of the ultrasound technique are that it is safe and eco-friendly. Ultrasound has been widely applied in areas such as medical examination and treatment, environmental governance, distance measurement, and surface cleaning [8–10]. With regards to the mineral flotation, the flotation efficiency was promoted by the ultrasonic treatment at an appropriate condition [11–13]. However, the physical and chemical effects of ultrasound are complicated. Different ultrasonic intensities and durations will lead to various results. Reasonable explanations for the ultrasonic effects have not been given from the perspectives of the ultrasonic mechanism. Further, previous studies focused only on the transient cavitation effect of ultrasound on flotation. The stable cavitation effect and acoustic radiation force effect also play important roles during ultrasonic treatment. However, there has been little research on these areas.

This review aims to clarify the reasons for ultrasonic effects. We aim to present a theoretical analysis for the improvement in the flotation process by ultrasonic treatment. In addition, we reviewed the application of ultrasonic treatment, and propose some directions for future research from the perspective of the ultrasonic mechanisms.

Cavitation and acoustic radiation force are two common effects existing in water during ultrasonic treatment. Cavitation is a phenomenon of nucleation that includes the behaviors of bubble dynamics: oscillation, growth, and collapse. However, the acoustic radiation force refers to the movement of particles, oil, or bubbles in a sound field. This section is reviewed from the theories of cavitation and acoustic radiation force.

The gas nuclei were reported to exist in water [14–16]. The common cavitation phenomenon is a type of heterogeneous cavitation, which is the growth process of bubbles from nuclei. In 1917, Rayleigh derived the equation of an ideal single bubble or a nucleus growing in an infinite domain liquid [17]. Then, Plesset first applied this equation to the movement of cavitation bubbles [18]. The modified equation later derived by Noltingk and Neppiras considering no temperature change during the cavitation process is given as follows [19,20]:

The four terms on the right side of the equation represent the surface tension term, inertial term, viscous term, and mass transfer term [21]. Eq. (1) is integrated numerically to obtain a strong nonlinearity solution. Fig. 1 shows the change in bubble size under a 20-kHz ultrasound plotted by Eq. (1). When the maximum pressure is set at 1 bar, the 1-μm-radius nuclei infinitely oscillate in the water without increasing in size. However, when the pressure reaches to 2 bar, the nuclei increases rapidly and then collapses within one period. This phenomenon is also known as transient cavitation (inertial cavitation) [22,23]. With the collapse of cavitation bubbles, a localized high temperature and high pressure are produced [21]. The collapse of nuclei is also the major cause of the cavitation effect.

From the previous section, the dynamic types of nuclei significantly change when the pressure increases. The term “cavitation threshold” is usually used to reflect the minimum required pressure for the change of bubble dynamic. Many models have been developed to predict the cavitation thresholds, which can be easily found in previous literature [14,24–26].

The bubble dynamics in the sound field can be divided into three types: oscillation, growth, and collapse. The minimum pressure for the change of bubble behavior from oscillation to growth is called the rectified diffusion threshold,

The bubble behaviors under different ultrasonic conditions can be predicted by the above cavitation thresholds. Fig. 2 is plotted according to Eqs. (2)–(4). The bubble at position X will grow and then collapse when it reaches the Blake threshold. In addition, bubbles at position Y will continually expand until they emerge from water. This process is also known as the degassing function of the ultrasonic bath [28].

The ultrasonic effect on bubbles will dramatically increase when the value of the bubble size is close to the resonance radius. The bubble resonance radius mainly depends on the frequency of the sound field. Leighton gave the relationship between the bubble resonance radius and ultrasonic frequency as follows [29]:

Bubbles will travel toward the pressure nodes when the bubble size is larger than the resonance radius, and toward the pressure antinodes when the bubble size is smaller than the resonance radius. According to Eqs. (6) and (7), the acoustic radiation forces are compared in Fig. 4 .

The cavitation effect and acoustic radiation force effect are two significant effects of ultrasound that occur in water. These two effects depend on various influence factors according to the ultrasonic mechanism (see Section 2). It should be noted that cavitation effects can be significantly changed at different frequencies and pressures. Based on the cavitation thresholds, the cavitation effects could be divided into two aspects: transient cavitation and stable cavitation. Fig. 5 shows the main impacts of ultrasound that can contribute to the flotation.

It is well-known that the low frequency (20–50 kHz) of ultrasound is beneficial for the transient cavitation. After the collapse of the transient cavitation bubbles, the high impact results in transient cavitation effects, e.g., the surface cleaning effect, sonochemistry effect, and breaking effect. The acoustic radiation force is evident in this frequency band. Bubbles would aggregate at the pressure nodes or antinodes owing to both the primary and secondary Bjerknes forces. The ultrasound has no apparent effect of force on particles compared to bubbles.

Furthermore, when the ultrasonic frequency increases, the intensity of transient cavitation becomes weak. Instead, the stable cavitation effect is enhanced. Stable bubbles are generated from nuclei on the particle surface. At a frequency of 0.2–1 MHz, the acoustic radiation force on bubbles was also reported to be more obvious than that on particles [32–35]. In addition, without collapse behavior, cavitation bubbles can drive the particles to aggregate at the pressure nodes or antinodes under moderate conditions. When the power of ultrasound was set at a higher value, the sonochemical efficiency will be greatly improved in this frequency band compared to low-frequency and high-frequency bands [36].

At a frequency of more than 1 MHz, the occurrence of cavitation behavior becomes quite difficult. Under these conditions, the acoustic radiation force on particles or oil droplets is relatively large (Fig. 4). Therefore, the acoustic radiation force could achieve precise manipulation on particles owing to the different acoustophoretic coefficient [37–39].

The transient cavitation effect includes a mechanical effect (such as surface cleaning effect and breaking effect) and chemical effect. When the sound pressure is higher than the transient cavitation threshold, the nuclei in water or on particle surfaces will grow and then collapse. After the collapse of cavitation bubbles, an intense physical shearing will be generated. Fig. 6 shows the transient cavitation effect on a large bubble and a kerosene droplet observed by a high-speed camera (Photron, FASTCAM Mini AX). The test was conducted by a high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU, Sonic Concepts H101). An ultrasonic pulse of five circles was produced at 1.1 MHz; the short-lived cavitation bubbles were generated at the focus of HIFU. The focused pressure was around 3 MPa, as measured by a hydrophone (Onda HHR-0500). The bubble and oil droplet were formed and attached on a springe needle. When cavitation bubbles collapse, the bubble and oil droplet are destroyed by a powerful jet flow. Owing to the transient cavitation effect, tiny bubbles are broken from the large bubble, and oil droplets are sufficiently dispersed. Furthermore, the hydrophobic minerals will be deeply cleaned when transient cavitation bubbles are formed on the surface of particles. Then, the fresh and hydrophobic surface is exposed, which becomes more easily collected by flotation.

In addition, the local temperature and pressure are dramatically increased after the transient cavitation, which was reported to reach 1000–5000 K and 100–50,000 bar [40–43]. Owing to the high temperature, the molecular bonds are broken into two free radical molecules with a free electron: water H
and OH
[44,45]. The free radicals can combine with each other, which results in a series of reactions [44]. This effect is called the chemical effect of ultrasound, and has been widely applied in chemical synthesis [46], food industry [47], and wastewater treatment [48].

The stable cavitation effect is the effect of the formation of tiny bubbles from nuclei. The transient cavitation effect will become weak when the frequency is increased [49]. When the sound pressure is between the transient cavitation threshold and rectified diffusion threshold, gas nuclei will grow up without collapse (see Fig. 2, position Y). A large bubble population is formed owing to the diffusion and coalescence [23,28]. Stable bubbles can be formed on the hydrophobic mineral surfaces to improve the floatability [50]. As shown in Fig. 7 , a large number of tiny bubbles with an average size of around 20 μm were produced owing to the stable cavitation effect. This test was carried out at a 600-kHz frequency using a plate transducer (BII-7583, Benthowave) assembled at the bottom of a glass container. The maximum liquid pressure in the container is around 100 kPa, as measured by a hydrophone. Furthermore, the size of stable cavitation bubbles is generally much smaller than the conventional flotation bubbles. The existence of the tiny bubble is beneficial to improve the collision efficiency with fine particles [51,52].

The acoustic radiation force effect is related to the movement of bubbles or particles. In the sound field, all of the particles, oil, and bubbles are affected by acoustic radiation forces. Under kHz-order ultrasonic standing waves, the bubbles would aggregate. We used a plate transducer (SX08-03, Sensor Technology Ltd.) to study the acoustic radiation force effect at 50 kHz. Nitrogen bubbles were generated by a glass frit in a square glass tube. The transducer was attached to the side of the glass tube, and the ultrasonic gel was applied to remove the air between the transducer and glass. Fig. 8 shows that bubbles aggregated when the ultrasound was turned on. The maximum pressure measured by the hydrophone is around 80 kPa. The bubbles aggregated fast when the ultrasound was turned on. Under the ultrasonic treatment at this frequency, the effects of the acoustic radiation force on bubbles are obvious. However, this effect has also been seldom discussed in previous reports. After aggregating, the flotation bubbles may obtain a large carrying capacity to collect minerals. In addition, the rise velocity of bubbles dramatically increased during ultrasound treatment (see the supporting information). This phenomenon is more likely to affect the flotation rate, but it has not been studied.

Under MHz-order ultrasonic standing waves, the particles and oil droplets could be precisely manipulated [31,53]. On the contrary, the acoustic radiation force on bubbles is sensitive to low-frequency ultrasound, and it dramatically decreases when the bubble size is much different from the resonance radius. Furthermore, the movement of the bubbles towards pressure nodes or antinodes can cause the aggregation of particles or oil droplets [32,35,53,54].
As shown in Fig. 9 , the ultrasonic devices are divided into three types owing to the different transducer shapes: horn type; plate type; and focused type.

The horn-type ultrasound is one of the most popular products. The probe of the transducer is between 5 mm and 15 mm [55,56]. A spherical wave is generated from the horn-type ultrasonic equipment so the ultrasound intensity varies with the transmission distance [57]. This type of ultrasound device easily generates large amounts of sound energy, which can reach 10 Atm [58]. High physical shear is generated owing to the process of transient cavitation, which is usually applied for the production of emulsion [59,60]. Horn-type ultrasound equipment is generally only applied in the laboratory, and it is challenging to produce the industrial-scale device as reported [61].

The plate ultrasonic equipment consists of several flat transducers, which are attached to a liquid container. The diameter of this transducer is much larger than the horn type, and the sound waves from this transducer are emitted in parallel. The laboratorial ultrasonic bath is a typical plate ultrasonic device that is employed for surface cleaning. Furthermore, in this case, it is usually more challenging for the sound waves to achieve high sound energy compared to the horn type [62]. However, the incident energy in the whole container is uniformly distributed [61]. Hence, the plate ultrasonic equipment was not only used for the surface cleaning, but is also applied for the acoustic manipulation of particles or oil droplets [35,63].

The ultrasound wave from the focused-type ultrasonic equipment is a convergent spherical wave. This kind of device can concentrate the sound waves at a focus point to achieve the precise control. It has been widely used in the medical field [64,65]. In addition, the transient cavitation behavior can be accurately studied [16,40]. In this paper, this type of ultrasonic device was used to demonstrate the transient cavitation effect.
The ultrasonic pretreatment (UPT) and simultaneous treatment (UST) are known as two common methods to improve minerals flotation (see Fig. 10 ).

The UPT technique is applied in the pre-conditioning process before flotation. The surface of the mineral particle will be deeply cleaned by the transient cavitation effect. In addition, added to the slurry during the preconditioning, flotation reagents are sufficiently dispersed to obtain a better attachment with the mineral surface. The application of an ultrasound before flotation can provide a moderate flow field for the rising of bubbles. Owing to the independent process from the flotation, the UPT method can easily adjust the ultrasonic treatment time and intensity. In particular, for the desulfuration of the coal sample using an ultrasound, high efficiency is achieved under long-time ultrasonic treatment.

For the UST flotation, the ultrasonic treatment and aeration processes are synchronously conducted. Compared to the UPT method, the effect of the ultrasound on the conventional flotation bubbles is the most apparent feature. The ultrasound will break the conventional flotation bubbles into a smaller size to improve the flotation of fine particles. However, the detachment probability between bubbles, reagent, and particles was also significantly increased when the ultrasound intensity is excessively large. This treatment was reported to be more useful for the mineral flotation, which required a chemical-absorption collector [66]. With respect to the physical-absorption collector, the mineralization of the mineral and collector can easily be destroyed owing to the low interaction force between the collector and mineral. Furthermore, it is difficult to control the treatment time, so the dispersion effect on flotation reagents may become weak compared to the UPT method.

During the flotation process, ultrafine clay or gangue minerals will adhere to the surface of valuable mineral, which is known as slime coating [67–69]. The hydrophilic “armor” is formed on the mineral surface, and this deteriorated the adhesion behavior between particles and bubbles or flotation reagents [1]. The slime coatings cause a harmful effect on the froth flotation [52,70]. Based on the cleaning effect of the transient cavitation, the slime coatings could be removed from the mineral surface.

Various research studies have reported that UPT is an effective method to alleviate the deterioration of slime coatings in the flotation process. Bandini studied the removal of slime from quartz surfaces (25–38 μm) using an ultrasonic bath [71]. The absorbance was determined using the UV–Vis NIR spectrophotometer to reflect the removal of slime coatings in the absence and presence of ultrasonic treatment. After being treated with sound waves at 150 W for 10 min, the fraction of removed slimes significantly increased from around 20% to 95% at pH 4.

It was proposed that the ultrasonic treatment was beneficial to the flotation of calcite and barite instead of quartz [66,72]. Gurpinar suggested that the improvement depends on the adsorption mechanism of the collector on particle surfaces [66]. A 40-kHz horn-type ultrasonic device was used for the UPT in his study. The surface of calcite and barite particles was cleaned by the transient cavitation effect. It is noted that the adsorption of sodium oleate collector on calcite and barite surfaces is due to chemical adsorption. The interaction forces involved in chemical adsorption is so strong that the cavitation effect can hardly break the adhesion of the collector on the calcite and barite surface. Meanwhile, in the quartz flotation process, the adsorption of the Armac T collector is based on physical interaction forces, and it is hence unstable under ultrasonic treatment.

Altun et al. studied the ultrasonic cleaning effect of the UPT method on the flotation performance of oil shale [73]. The flotation of −150-μm oil shale was treated using a horn-type ultrasound device with 20% to 100% of 250 W total power. The SEM results indicated that the fine inorganic entities were liberated from pores and cavities of concentrate surface after ultrasonic treatment (in Fig. 11 ). In addition, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the concentration indicated that the inorganic constituent was significantly decreased. As a result, the ash contents of two oil shale samples by ultrasonic treatment were reduced by 23% and 35%.

The 25- and 40-kHz plate-type ultrasound equipment was used to improve the flotation of coal [74–77]. The flotation of coal (−0.5 mm) under UST was conducted using four plate transducers at 50 W in the presence of a 100-g/t reagent dosage [74–76]. Ozkan found that the ash content of tailings drastically decreased by performing ultrasonic treatment [75]. This indicated that the gangue was removed from the coal surface so that the entrainment effect of slime was restrained. As a result, the combustible recovery increased by around 35%. The UST method was also used to enhance the flotation recovery of copper from copper sulfide tailings [12]. Ultrasonic treatment was conducted using nine plate ultrasonic transducers at 100 W and 20 kHz. The recovery of copper was increased by 3.5% with UST.

With respect to the flotation of coal or sulfide, the oxidation film on particle surfaces was an important reason for the low flotation efficiency. In particular, for coal samples, particles may be oxidized during transportation and storage processes [3,78]. The hydrophilic oxygen functional groups were formed on the surface of coal, such as carboxyl, phenolic, and carbonyl [2,3,78]. As a result, the adhesion probability was decreased, and the detachment probability was increased owing to the decreased hydrophobicity of the coal surface; hence, the flotation efficiency was declined. To solve this problem, the ultrasound was employed to remove the oxidation film from the particle surface.

Misra et al. found that UPT using an ultrasonic bath was an effective method to improve the flotation recovery of arsenopyrite [79]. The flotation of 38–120-μm arsenopyrite sample was conducted using a modified Hallimond tube. After the long-time sonication, the sign of zeta-potential values changed from negative to positive. The oxidation film was supposed to be removed so that the arsenopyrite became floatable after the ultrasonic treatment.

Cao et al. studied the flotation of oxidized pyrite using the UST method [80]. The 28-kHz ultrasonic transducer with a power up to 100 W was attached to the bottom of the flotation cell. The pyrite samples were oxidized in H2O2 solution for 12 min and 20 min. In Fig. 12 , after ultrasonic treatment with 0.9-W/cm2 energy density for 20 s, the contact angle of pyrite was improved by about 30°. Moreover, it indicated that the effect of the ultrasound on the oxidation film was determined by the ultrasonication time and intensity. Long-time ultrasonic treatment showed a negative result on the hydrophobicity of oxidized pyrite. The iron recovery was improved from 61.09% to 81.03% when the ultrasound was successively applied in the rougher stage (75-W power) and cleaner stage (50-W power).

Feng and Aldrich compared the cleaning effect of ultrasound and high-intensity conditioning (HIC) on oxidized coal [81]. The −0.5-mm coal samples were pretreated by an ultrasonic bath at 20 kHz and 120 W. The humic acid was found to be degraded into soluble acids after the ultrasonic treatment. Therefore, the H+ was released into the solution, and the pH of the slurry would decrease if the oxidation film is removed from the particle surface. The flotation results indicated that the cleaning effect of the ultrasound on the oxidation film was much better than for the HIC method. The recovery improved by around 15% after 4 min of UPT.

Xu et al. analyzed the XPS results to determine the ultrasonic cleaning effect of oxidation film on coal surfaces [82]. The coal samples, which were oxidized in 30% H2O2 solution for 24 h, were pretreated by a 40-kHz UGD multifunction ultrasonic transmitter for 0 min, 3 min, 5 min, 10 min, and 20 min. In Fig. 13 , the ratio C–C/C–H is seen to increase after ultrasonic time within 10 min, which indicated that the fresh coal surfaces were exposed, and the oxidation films were removed by ultrasonic treatment. In addition, the results from the flotation column tests indicated that the maximum improvement (15%) in the recovery occurred after ultrasonic treatment of 10 min. It was reported that the ultrasound could eliminate the oxidation films on the particle surface, but an excessive ultrasonic treatment time would cause the reoxidized effect [3,81,82].

Many reports have indicated that ultrasound could both remove and generate the oxidation film on mineral surfaces [3,80–82] (see Figs. 12 and 13). It should be noted that the cleaning effect by ultrasonic treatment is a physical or mechanical behavior, but the sonochemistry effect is a chemical behavior. With respect to the physical effect, the cavitation probability and intensity determine the efficiency of the cleaning effect. However, the chemical effect is not obvious during the initial period. The sonochemistry effect requires a longer time to be activated compared to the cleaning effect. As shown in Fig. 14 , the highest flotation efficiency would occur at an optimal time for ultrasonic treatment. However, after the long-time ultrasonic treatment, the particle surface would be oxidized again, and the flotation efficiency would deteriorate.

As it is an unclean fuel, it is not permitted to burn a coal sample with high sulfur components. The challenge of desulfurization from coal was always expected to be solved during the flotation process. From this perspective, the ultrasound was reported to be beneficial to the desulphurization of high-sulfur coal (sulfur content is more than 3%) flotation [11,83–88]. After the collapse of cavitation bubbles, free radical molecules were formed in water. The sulfur could be resolved from the coal particles through complex chemical reactions.

Kang et al. used a 20-kHz horn-type ultrasonic transmitter to pretreat the coal sample with a sulfur content of 3.82% at 200 W [86]. After ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, the perfect indexes of flotation and desulphurization were increased by 22.51% and 25.36%, respectively. In addition, Kang et al. investigated the ultrasonic desulphurization effect using XRD analysis, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy [11]. The results revealed that the pyrite, which is in the form of FeS, could be separated from the coal sample by UPT. Because of the sonochemistry effect, the oxidation effect on the pyrite surface by the radical molecules resulted in the formation of sulphoxide [83]. It was proposed that ferric hydroxide be coated on the pyrite surface, resulting in a restriction on the floatability of pyrite. Furthermore, it was proven that the sonoelectrochemical method enhances the desulfuration in the flotation of high sulfur coal [89–91]. The electron transfer efficiency was improved by performing ultrasonic treatment. After a combination of the electrochemical action at 15 × 10−3 A/cm2 and sonochemical action at 50 kHz and 1.2 W/cm2 for 20 min, the sulfur reduction reached 75.4% [91].

Talc is a common gangue in sulphide ores and platinum group ores [92–94]. The specific structure makes the talc particles hydrophobic, which was reported to result in contamination in flotation concentrates [92]. Feng and Aldrich studied the ultrasonication effect on the column flotation of talc [94]. The UPT tests were conducted by a horn-type ultrasonic setup at 30 kHz with a 90-W/cm2 power density. After 4 min of UPT, the recovery of the 25–53-μm talc sample reduced from around 70% to 40%. It was suggested that the formation of the magnesium and silicon hydroxyl by the chemistry effect was the main reason for this reduction in flotation recovery.

During the process of UST flotation, the bubble size in froth was reported to be smaller [80,95–97]. Fig. 15 showed the images of the froth during the UST flotation, in which the bubble size in the froth dramatically decreased [95]. It is well-known that the collision efficiency of fine particles is inversely proportional to the bubble size [4,5]. Therefore, an improvement in flotation recovery would be obtained in the presence of smaller bubbles owing to the ultrasonic treatment.

The formation of the smaller bubbles during the ultrasonic treatment may result from two factors: 1) stable cavitation bubbles generated from the nuclei, and 2) small bubbles burst from the conventional large bubbles owing to the breaking effect.

Tiny bubbles will be generated by stable cavitation at medium frequency (see Fig. 8). When the liquid pressure is lower than the transient cavitation threshold but large than the rectified diffusion threshold, the nuclei would grow and coalesce into tiny bubbles. However, over the past few decades, most studies focused on the applications of ultrasound at low frequency. The benefit of the tiny bubbles formed by stable cavitation has seldom been discussed.

Under low-frequency ultrasound, it is difficult to generate stable cavitation bubbles. As proposed in Fig. 2, stable cavitation bubbles can only be produced from the large nuclei (>1 μm) at 50 kHz. The small nuclei will violently collapse within several cycles when the sound pressure is greater than the transient cavitation threshold. However, there are insufficient large gas nuclei in water under normal conditions. Therefore, the stable cavitation bubbles from the large nuclei in water should not be the main reason for the small froth shown in Fig. 15. Furthermore, this phenomenon mainly appeared during the UST flotation, but was seldom observed after UPT. This indicated that the air-inflation process during the UST flotation should be related to the small froth. The conventional flotation bubbles (>1 mm) are formed during the air-inflation process. According to Eq. (7), an extraordinarily higher transient cavitation threshold was required for bubbles whose sizes are much larger than the resonance radius. In other words, conventional flotation bubbles can hardly grow and collapse under ultrasonic action. Instead, conventional flotation bubbles could be broken owing to transient cavitation from small nuclei in water, which should be the primary cause for the tiny froth during the UST flotation.

From Fig. 6, the transient cavitation bubbles were generated around the oil droplet. After the collapse of transient cavitation bubbles, a large number of tiny oil droplets were separated from a large droplet. According to this breaking effect, the emulsions or sufficiently dispersed reagents can be produced by ultrasonic treatment with an appropriate ultrasonic intensity and time. The small distribution of reagents after ultrasonic treatment is beneficial to the adsorption on the particle surface. Hence, the consumption of the reagent in flotation decreases dramatically.

Celik investigated the flotation of galena with the UPT method using a Bransonic model ultrasonic cleaner [98]. The flotation was conducted in a modified Hallimond tube, and the potassium ethyl xanthate was applied for the collector. The flotation results indicated that the consumption of the collector could be reduced by at least 50% under appropriate ultrasonic conditions.

Ozkan and Kuyumcu determined that the collector required for the flotation of coal samples dramatically decreased after UST (25 and 40 kHz, 50 W for each transducer) [74]. The results showed that the combustible recovery increased by 20%–35% with a low reagent dosage (100 g/l) after ultrasonic treatment. An ultrasonic cleaning bath (40 kHz, 600 W power) was employed to improve the flotation efficiency of the zinc from the lead–zinc–copper ore [99]. With 5-min ultrasonic pretreatment, the grade and recovery increased from 29.41% to 39.97%, and from 16.93% to 24.51%, respectively. Kursun and Ulusoy considered that sufficient dispersion by ultrasound resulted in an improvement in the adsorption of the collector on mineral [99]. The above-mentioned studies indicated that ultrasonic pretreatment in the presence of the collector may achieve better flotation results than the one in the absence of the collector.

The aggregation of particles, oils, or bubbles have been widely studied in recent years [31,53,100]. The acoustic radiation force on particles and oil in ultrasonic standing-wave (USW) fields could be precisely controlled. USW has been an advanced technology for the aggregation of particles in micro-channels, such as cell separation [101,102].

The aggregation of particles in water could be achieved by the manipulation of bubbles or particles. In a kHz-order frequency USW field, an aggregation behavior of coal particles was proposed in our previous research [32]. The flotation tests of a 1-g/l coal particle (74–125 μm, 5% ash content) suspension were conducted using a USW aggregation-flotation technology at 200 kHz. Fig. 16 shows the aggregation phenomenon recorded by a CCD camera and a microscope. Stable cavitation bubbles are generated on the particle surface and gathered at the pressure nodes, and then the bubbles connected with the particles to form the aggregates. After the aggregation of coal particles by USW pretreatment, the flotation rate markedly improved. The flotation recovery increased from 57% to 68% at a flotation time of 2 min.

The aggregation effect of the bubble under a kHz-order ultrasound has seldom been discussed in previous reports. However, this effect should also be an essential factor affecting the flotation efficiency during ultrasonic treatment. As shown in Fig. 8, the bubbles were aggregated by the effect of the acoustic radiation force at low frequency. The aggregation behavior of bubbles would significantly influence the flotation process, and should not be ignored. The large aggregates of bubbles may be attributed to the flotation of minerals.

However, oil droplets could also aggregate owing to the acoustic radiation force in the USW field. USW has been considered as an efficient technology for the separation of oil and water [37–39]. For some oily minerals, such as bitumen, the aggregation effect of ultrasound may also improve flotation efficiency. There has been insufficient research on the USW aggregation-flotation technology, but this technology has shown a promising prospect for the future.

The use of ultrasound technology is promising for the improvement of the flotation efficiency. In this review, we analyzed the ultrasonic effects in detail, including the transient cavitation effect, stable cavitation effect, and acoustic radiation force effect. The phenomena formed by these effects were clearly observed using a high-speed camera and a CCD camera. Furthermore, the characteristics of different ultrasonic equipment and treatment methods were discussed. Finally, the applications of ultrasound in flotation were reviewed and discussed.

Previous studies on the application of ultrasound to flotation fields are insufficient. Over the past few decades, many studies have discussed the transient cavitation effect on the mineral flotation, such as surface cleaning. However, the stable cavitation effect and acoustic radiation force effect may also occur during ultrasonic treatment. Fewer studies have mentioned or discussed these two effects. In this paper, we demonstrated these two effects by performing some simple tests, and the results are expected to provide direction for future research. In addition, the aggregation of minerals by USW owing to both the stable cavitation effect and acoustic radiation force effect is a promising way of improving the flotation efficiency. More studies can be conducted on this acoustic aggregation method.
